twitter.hook: In 1995, the CIA officially shut down Project Stargate, saying remote viewing never produced 'actionable intelligence.' Three months earlier, they had already made that decision — before the evaluation results even arrived.
thread: The program ran for 23 years under five code names: SCANATE, GRILL FLAME, CENTER LANE, SUN STREAK, STARGATE. Each rename required continued funding justification. 19 intelligence agencies used it. The customer return rate was 89.5%.

Remote Viewer #001 Joseph McMoneagle was used for 450+ missions. In 1984, he received the Legion of Merit — one of the U.S. military's highest honors — for providing intelligence 'unavailable from any other source.' Read that phrase carefully.

In 1979, a Soviet Tu-22 reconnaissance aircraft crashed in Zaire. Satellite surveillance failed. The CIA called viewer Rosemary Smith. Given only a map of Africa, she marked the crash site. The aircraft was found within 3 miles. President Carter publicly confirmed it in 1995.

Viewer Pat Price was given coordinates intended for a CIA agent's summer cabin. Instead, he described a nearby classified NSA listening post — correctly naming personnel and reading code words from file folders. The accuracy triggered a Pentagon security investigation.
McMoneagle described a never-before-seen Soviet submarine design — the twin-hulled Typhoon class — at an Arctic shipyard. Before satellite confirmation. That's in the public record.

The 1995 AIR evaluation: two reviewers, two verdicts. UC Davis statistician Jessica Utts concluded psychic functioning was 'well established by scientific standards' with p-values below 10⁻²⁰. Skeptic Ray Hyman agreed effects were 'too large to dismiss as flukes' but called conclusions premature.
The CIA officially closed the program on June 30, 1995. The evaluation results weren't received until three months later. The decision to close preceded the evidence meant to justify it.

Three explanations. Option 1: it worked, and they don't want it known. Option 2: it worked inconsistently — useful enough to fund, too unreliable to admit publicly. Option 3: the official closure was official. Not actual.
The honest position: we don't know. The architecture of classified programs means we may never know. What the declassified documents do tell us: the official narrative doesn't match the evidence.
23 years. $20 million. 19 agencies. 89.5% return rate. A Legion of Merit citation. And an official conclusion that it never worked. Hold those two facts in the same hand.
twitter.cta: Read the full declassified file: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/government-programs/stargate-project — If it didn't work, why did they keep using it? Share this if the answer matters to you.

linkedin.hook:
The U.S. government spent $20 million and 23 years funding a program to develop 'psychic spies' — then closed it while claiming it never produced actionable intelligence.

The same archive that contains that conclusion also contains a Legion of Merit citation for 'intelligence unavailable from any other source.'

Both are real. Both are documented.
linkedin.body:
Project Stargate is, on the surface, easy to dismiss: a Cold War-era program chasing fringe science, terminated in 1995 after a review found no actionable results.

But the declassified documents tell a more complicated story.

The program ran continuously from 1972 to 1995 under five code names — each requiring renewed justification for continued funding. Nineteen intelligence agencies used its remote-viewing services during that period. The customer return rate was 89.5 percent. You don't sustain that return rate for two decades with a product that doesn't work.

Remote Viewer #001, Joseph McMoneagle, received the Legion of Merit in 1984 for providing 'critical intelligence reported at the highest echelons of our military and government, including the Joint Chiefs of Staff, DIA, NSA, CIA, DEA, and the Secret Service — intelligence unavailable from any other source.' That citation is in the public record.

The 1995 evaluation produced two verdicts from two reviewers: UC Davis statistician Jessica Utts concluded psychic functioning was 'well established' using standard scientific criteria, with p-values below 10⁻²⁰. Ray Hyman, the skeptic reviewer, agreed the effects were too large and consistent to be dismissed as flukes, but called final conclusions premature.

The CIA officially closed the program three months before receiving those results.

I'm not claiming remote viewing is real. I'm pointing at a structural pattern that shows up repeatedly in how government-funded consciousness research gets handled: evidence accumulates, then gets filed in a way that makes the official narrative cleaner than the data warrants.

The question worth sitting with isn't 'does remote viewing work?' It's: what does it tell us about institutions when the decision to close precedes the evidence meant to justify it?

Full documented file: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/government-programs/stargate-project

slides.title: SLIDE 1 — HOOK
slides.body: The CIA spent $20 million and 23 years on 'psychic spies.' Then said it never worked. The same archive says otherwise.

slides.title: SLIDE 2 — STAT
slides.body: 19 intelligence agencies used Project Stargate. Customer return rate: 89.5%. Programs don't survive 23 years because they produce nothing.

slides.title: SLIDE 3 — REVEAL
slides.body: Remote Viewer #001 Joseph McMoneagle received the Legion of Merit in 1984 for intelligence 'unavailable from any other source' — the U.S. military's formal citation for a man doing something they later said didn't work.

slides.title: SLIDE 4 — DETAIL
slides.body: UC Davis statistician Jessica Utts reviewed the 1995 data: 'Using the standards applied to any other area of science, psychic functioning has been well established.' P-values below 10⁻²⁰. The CIA closed the program 3 months before receiving her report.

slides.title: SLIDE 5 — QUOTE
slides.body: President Carter, 1995: 'We focused our satellite cameras on that point and the plane was there.' — Confirming viewer Rosemary Smith located a crashed Soviet aircraft in Zaire within 3 miles using only a map.

slides.title: SLIDE 6 — IMPLICATION
slides.body: The decision to close preceded the evidence meant to justify it. That's not a conspiracy. That's the documented timeline.

slides.title: SLIDE 7 — SOURCES
slides.body: CIA CREST archive (12M+ declassified pages) · AIR 1995 evaluation · McMoneagle Legion of Merit citation · Jessica Utts, 'An Assessment of the Evidence for Psychic Functioning' (1995)

slides.title: SLIDE 8 — CTA
slides.body: Read the full declassified file at blackswanp.com/research/government-programs/stargate-project

reddit.title: The CIA closed Project Stargate 3 months before receiving the evaluation results — what does the actual declassified record show?
reddit.body:
Been going through the CIA CREST archive on Stargate and wanted to share some specifics that don't make it into most discussions.

The official line is that the program was terminated in 1995 after an AIR-commissioned review found it produced no actionable intelligence. That's the story most people know.

What's in the actual documents:

The program ran under five sequential code names from 1972 to 1995 (SCANATE, GRILL FLAME, CENTER LANE, SUN STREAK, STARGATE). 19 intelligence agencies used the service. Customer return rate was 89.5%.

Joseph McMoneagle received the Legion of Merit in 1984 — a formal military citation stating he provided intelligence 'unavailable from any other source' to the Joint Chiefs, DIA, NSA, CIA, DEA, and Secret Service.

President Carter publicly confirmed in 1995 that remote viewer Rosemary Smith located a crashed Soviet Tu-22 in Zaire within 3 miles using only a map of Africa, when satellite surveillance had failed.

The 1995 AIR evaluation: two reviewers. Jessica Utts (UC Davis statistician) concluded psychic functioning was 'well established' using standard scientific criteria — p-values below 10⁻²⁰. Ray Hyman agreed the effects were too large to be statistical flukes, but called conclusions premature.

Critical timeline detail: the CIA officially closed the program on June 30, 1995. The evaluation results were not received until three months later. The closure decision preceded the evidence meant to justify it.

I put together a full writeup with primary document citations here: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/government-programs/stargate-project

I'm not claiming remote viewing is definitively real — the evidence is messy and the negative cases are real too. But the official 'it never worked' narrative genuinely doesn't match the internal documents. The 89.5% return rate and the Legion of Merit citation alone are hard to square with the official closure statement.

reddit.title: Project Stargate documentation thread: what's actually in the 12 million declassified CIA pages, vs. what people claim is in them
reddit.body:
There's a lot of noise around Stargate — claims that get exaggerated, details that get dropped, and the actual primary documents that most people haven't read.

Here's what I found when I went through the CIA CREST archive directly:

DOCUMENTED FACTS:
- Program ran 1972–1995 under five code names, costing approximately $20 million
- 19 intelligence agencies used the service with an 89.5% customer return rate
- McMoneagle's Legion of Merit citation (1984) specifically cites intelligence 'unavailable from any other source' to named agencies
- Carter publicly confirmed the Zaire Tu-22 case in 1995 ('We focused our satellite cameras on that point and the plane was there')
- The 1995 AIR evaluation found subjects scoring 5–15% above chance with p < 10⁻²⁰
- The CIA formally closed the program June 30, 1995 — three months before receiving the AIR evaluation results

WHAT'S GENUINELY UNCERTAIN:
- Whether the operational successes reflect actual psi vs. good contextual guessing
- Whether the 'official closure' represented a full program termination or a public-facing discontinuation
- The Pat Price NSA facility case — what's in the file is partially redacted
- How to reconcile the 89.5% return rate with 'no actionable intelligence produced'

I've been building out a documented breakdown here: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/government-programs/stargate-project

The honest position: the official narrative doesn't match the available evidence. That's not the same as 'remote viewing is real.' It's pointing at a gap that the documents themselves create.

What do people here think is the most credible explanation for the gap between the Legion of Merit citation and the official 'it didn't work' conclusion?

email.subject: They closed it 3 months before the results arrived
email.preheader: 23 years. $20 million. 89.5% return rate. Then: 'no actionable intelligence produced.'
email.body:
In 1984, Joseph McMoneagle received the Legion of Merit for intelligence 'unavailable from any other source' — provided to the Joint Chiefs, DIA, NSA, and CIA through remote viewing.

In 1995, the CIA officially closed Project Stargate — three months before receiving the evaluation results they'd commissioned to justify the decision.

The same AIR review found subjects scoring 5–15% above chance with p-values below 10⁻²⁰. UC Davis statistician Jessica Utts called it 'well established by scientific standards.' The skeptic reviewer agreed effects were too large to dismiss as flukes.

19 agencies. 89.5% return rate. A Legion of Merit citation. And an official conclusion that it never worked.

The full declassified file: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/government-programs/stargate-project

Subscribe for one transmission per week. The rest of the archive is in there.

pullQuotes: The CIA closed Stargate 3 months before receiving the evaluation it commissioned.
89.5% customer return rate. 19 agencies. 23 years. 'It didn't work.'
McMoneagle's Legion of Merit: intelligence 'unavailable from any other source.'
The official narrative doesn't match the evidence. That's the documented record.
President Carter: 'We focused our satellite cameras on that point and the plane was there.'
